content.text: Managed Identities & RBAC

Azure Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is the authorization layer that determines what a managed identity is permitted to do after it obtains a token. A role assignment binds three things together: the managed identity (who), a role definition (what actions are allowed), and a scope (which resources the actions apply to). Without at least one role assignment, a managed identity token is valid but every API call returns 403 Forbidden.

attrs.alt: Azure RBAC components: Security Principal (Managed Identity) + Role Definition + Scope = Role Assignment

content.text: The Three Components of a Role Assignment
Security Principal

For managed identities, the security principal is the Entra ID service principal that Azure creates when the identity is enabled. Its unique identifier is the principal ID (also called the object ID), which you target when creating role assignments. The principal ID is different from the client ID — use principal ID for RBAC assignments.

# Get principal ID of a system-assigned identity on a VM
az vm identity show \
    --resource-group MyRG --name MyVM \
    --query principalId --output tsv

# Get principal ID of a user-assigned identity
az identity show \
    --resource-group MyRG --name my-shared-identity \
    --query principalId --output tsv
Role Definition

A role definition specifies a set of allowed Actions (control-plane operations like read/write resource metadata) and DataActions (data-plane operations like reading blob contents). Built-in roles cover most scenarios. Custom roles allow you to construct a minimal permission set.
Built-in Role
Actions / DataActions Allowed
Common Use Case
Key Vault Secrets User
Read secret values (data-plane)
App reads connection strings from Key Vault
Key Vault Crypto User
Sign / verify / encrypt / decrypt (data-plane)
App performs cryptographic operations
Storage Blob Data Reader
Read blob contents and metadata
App reads files from a storage container
Storage Blob Data Contributor
Read + write + delete blobs
App uploads reports to storage
Storage Queue Data Contributor
Peek / receive / send / delete queue messages
Worker processes a message queue
Azure Service Bus Data Receiver
Receive + peek messages
Consumer reads from a Service Bus queue
Azure Service Bus Data Sender
Send messages
Producer publishes to a Service Bus topic
Cosmos DB Built-in Data Reader
Read documents and containers (Cosmos RBAC)
App queries Cosmos DB
Contributor
Create and manage most resources (control-plane)
Identity manages Azure resources directly
Scope

The scope determines which resources the role assignment covers. Assigning at a broader scope (subscription) grants access to all child resources. Always assign at the narrowest scope sufficient for the use case to limit blast radius if the identity is compromised.
Management Group — applies to all subscriptions under the group
Subscription — applies to all resource groups and resources in the subscription
Resource Group — applies to all resources in the group
Resource — applies only to that specific resource (preferred for least privilege)
Assigning Roles with Azure CLI

az role assignment create creates the role assignment in Azure ARM. The command accepts either the role name or its GUID. Use --assignee-object-id with --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal rather than --assignee (which resolves via Graph API and may fail for identities not yet propagated).

PRINCIPAL_ID=$(az vm identity show \
    --resource-group MyRG --name MyVM \
    --query principalId --output tsv)

# Assign Key Vault Secrets User on a specific Key Vault
KV_ID=$(az keyvault show --name MyVault --query id --output tsv)

az role assignment create \
    --assignee-object-id "$PRINCIPAL_ID" \
    --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
    --role 'Key Vault Secrets User' \
    --scope "$KV_ID"

# Assign Storage Blob Data Reader on a specific container
CONTAINER_SCOPE="/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/MyRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/myaccount/blobServices/default/containers/mycontainer"

az role assignment create \
    --assignee-object-id "$PRINCIPAL_ID" \
    --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
    --role 'Storage Blob Data Reader' \
    --scope "$CONTAINER_SCOPE"
Assigning Roles with Azure PowerShell

# Get principal ID
$principalId = (Get-AzWebApp -ResourceGroupName MyRG -Name MyApp).Identity.PrincipalId

# Get Key Vault resource ID
$kvId = (Get-AzKeyVault -VaultName MyVault).ResourceId

# Assign role
New-AzRoleAssignment `
    -ObjectId $principalId `
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Key Vault Secrets User' `
    -Scope $kvId

# Verify assignment
Get-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $principalId | Select-Object RoleDefinitionName, Scope
Assigning Roles with Terraform

Terraform's azurerm_role_assignment resource creates role assignments declaratively. Referencing the identity's principal_id from the same resource creation makes the dependency explicit and ensures correct ordering.
Custom Role Definitions

When built-in roles are too broad, create a custom role that allows only the specific actions your identity needs. Custom roles are scoped to a management group or subscription and can be assigned to any principal within that scope.

{
  "Name": "Blob Uploader — Output Container Only",
  "Description": "Can write and list blobs in the output container; cannot delete or read other containers.",
  "Actions": [],
  "NotActions": [],
  "DataActions": [
    "Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices/containers/blobs/write",
    "Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices/containers/blobs/add/action",
    "Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices/containers/blobs/read"
  ],
  "NotDataActions": [],
  "AssignableScopes": [
    "/subscriptions/aaaabbbb-cccc-dddd-eeee-ffffffffffff"
  ]
}

# Create the custom role from a JSON file
az role definition create --role-definition custom-role.json

# Then assign it like any built-in role
az role assignment create \
    --assignee-object-id "$PRINCIPAL_ID" \
    --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
    --role 'Blob Uploader — Output Container Only' \
    --scope "$CONTAINER_SCOPE"
Propagation Delay

Role assignments take effect within seconds in most cases, but the Azure documentation guarantees up to 5 minutes for propagation. In automation pipelines, add a wait or retry loop after creating a role assignment before the identity first uses the resource.

#!/bin/bash
# Assign role then wait for propagation before testing access
az role assignment create \
    --assignee-object-id "$PRINCIPAL_ID" \
    --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
    --role 'Key Vault Secrets User' \
    --scope "$KV_ID"

echo 'Waiting for role assignment to propagate...'
RETRIES=10
for i in $(seq 1 $RETRIES); do
    RESULT=$(az keyvault secret show \
        --vault-name MyVault --name test-secret 2>&1)
    if echo "$RESULT" | grep -q '"value"'; then
        echo 'Access confirmed.'
        break
    fi
    echo "  Attempt $i/$RETRIES — waiting 30s..."
    sleep 30
done
Listing and Auditing Role Assignments

# List all role assignments for a managed identity
PRINCIPAL_ID='aaaabbbb-cccc-dddd-eeee-ffffffffffff'

az role assignment list \
    --assignee "$PRINCIPAL_ID" \
    --all \
    --query '[].{Role:roleDefinitionName, Scope:scope}' \
    --output table

# List all managed identity role assignments in a resource group
az role assignment list \
    --resource-group MyRG \
    --query "[?principalType=='ServicePrincipal'].{Principal:principalName, Role:roleDefinitionName, Scope:scope}" \
    --output table
KQL: Role Assignment Changes

// Audit role assignment creation and deletion events
// Covers managed identity assignments (principalType = ServicePrincipal)
AzureActivity
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where OperationNameValue in (
    'MICROSOFT.AUTHORIZATION/ROLEASSIGNMENTS/WRITE',
    'MICROSOFT.AUTHORIZATION/ROLEASSIGNMENTS/DELETE'
  )
| extend Props = parse_json(Properties)
| extend
    RoleName     = tostring(Props.roleDefinitionName),
    AssigneeName = tostring(Props.principalName),
    Scope        = tostring(Props.scope),
    Caller       = Caller
| project TimeGenerated, OperationNameValue, RoleName, AssigneeName, Scope, Caller, ActivityStatus
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Removing Role Assignments

When a managed identity no longer needs access to a resource — or when a system-assigned identity is disabled — its role assignments should be removed. Stale role assignments increase the attack surface and are flagged by Microsoft Defender for Cloud.

# Remove a specific role assignment by ID
ASSIGNMENT_ID=$(az role assignment list \
    --assignee "$PRINCIPAL_ID" \
    --scope "$KV_ID" \
    --query '[0].id' --output tsv)

az role assignment delete --ids "$ASSIGNMENT_ID"

# Remove all role assignments for a principal across all scopes
az role assignment list --assignee "$PRINCIPAL_ID" --all \
    --query '[].id' --output tsv | \
xargs -I{} az role assignment delete --ids {}
Conditions on Role Assignments (ABAC)

Azure supports Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) conditions on role assignments. A condition is a boolean expression evaluated at authorization time that further restricts when the role applies — for example, allowing blob read only on blobs with a specific tag value. ABAC conditions are additive: they narrow built-in role permissions rather than expanding them.
Storage ABAC Example — Restrict by Blob Index Tag

# Allow blob read only when the blob has tag 'environment' = 'production'
CONDITION='(
  (
    !(ActionMatches{"Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices/containers/blobs/read"})
  )
  OR
  (
    @Resource[Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices/containers/blobs/tags:environment<\$key>]
    StringEquals "production"
  )
)'

az role assignment create \
    --assignee-object-id "$PRINCIPAL_ID" \
    --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
    --role 'Storage Blob Data Reader' \
    --scope "$STORAGE_ID" \
    --condition "$CONDITION" \
    --condition-version '2.0'
ABAC conditions are currently supported for Azure Storage (blob index tags) and Key Vault (key and secret attributes). Support for other resource types is being added incrementally.
Deny Assignments

Unlike role assignments that grant access, deny assignments explicitly block specific actions for a principal regardless of any role assignments that would otherwise permit them. Deny assignments are created by Azure Blueprints and certain managed applications — they cannot be created directly by users. Understanding them matters when a managed identity gets Forbidden despite having a valid role.
Deny assignments take precedence over role assignments — a deny always wins.
They are created by Azure Blueprints locked assignments and by some Azure Marketplace managed applications.
You can view deny assignments in the Azure portal under IAM > Deny assignments.
If a managed identity's role assignment looks correct but access is still denied, check for a deny assignment at a broader scope.

# List deny assignments for a principal
az rest \
    --method GET \
    --uri "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/denyAssignments?api-version=2022-04-01&%24filter=principalId+eq+'{principalId}'" \
    --query 'value[].{Name:properties.denyAssignmentName, Scope:properties.scope, DeniedActions:properties.permissions[0].actions}' \
    --output table
Role Assignment at Scale with Azure Policy

Azure Policy can automatically assign roles to managed identities when resources are created or remediated. The deployIfNotExists effect deploys a role assignment as part of the remediation task, ensuring every new resource's identity receives the required permissions without manual intervention.
Role Assignment Limits
Limit
Value
Notes
Max role assignments per subscription
Soft limit; can request increase from Azure Support
Max custom role definitions per directory
Across all subscriptions in the Entra ID tenant
Max assignable scopes per custom role
Number of scopes listed in AssignableScopes
Max conditions per role assignment
One condition expression per assignment
Propagation time
Up to 5 minutes
Typically seconds; guaranteed SLA is 5 minutes

The 4000 role assignment limit per subscription is a hard architectural limit. In large environments where many identities need the same access, prefer user-assigned identities (one identity shared by many resources = one role assignment instead of many).
Troubleshooting 403 Forbidden Errors
A 403 from a service means the identity's token was accepted (authentication succeeded) but the authorization check failed. Work through this checklist systematically:

# 1. Confirm the identity is enabled and get its principal ID
az vm identity show --resource-group RG --name VM --query principalId -o tsv

# 2. List current role assignments for the principal
az role assignment list --assignee "$PRINCIPAL_ID" --all \
    --query '[].{Role:roleDefinitionName, Scope:scope}' -o table

# 3. Confirm the assignment covers the target resource's scope
# (assignment at RG scope covers child resources; at resource scope only that resource)

# 4. Check for deny assignments
az rest --method GET \
  --uri "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{sub}/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/denyAssignments?api-version=2022-04-01"

# 5. For Key Vault — check if vault uses access policies (not RBAC)
az keyvault show --name MyVault --query properties.enableRbacAuthorization
# If false: vault uses access policies, RBAC assignments have no effect

# 6. Check if there is a Conditional Access policy blocking token issuance
# (rare for managed identities but possible with strict CA rules)
GitHub Actions — Manage Role Assignments in CI/CD

When deploying infrastructure through GitHub Actions, the workflow credential (service principal or OIDC identity) needs the User Access Administrator role at the target scope to create role assignments on behalf of deployed managed identities. This is a common requirement for fully automated infrastructure pipelines.

# .github/workflows/deploy-infra.yml
name: Deploy Infrastructure

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ['infra/**']

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Azure Login (OIDC)
        uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
          tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
          subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}

      - name: Deploy App Service + Identity
        run: |
          az deployment group create \
            --resource-group MyRG \
            --template-file infra/main.bicep \
            --parameters @infra/params.json

      - name: Assign Role to Deployed Identity
        run: |
          PRINCIPAL_ID=$(az webapp identity show \
            --resource-group MyRG --name MyApp \
            --query principalId --output tsv)

          KV_ID=$(az keyvault show --name MyVault --query id --output tsv)

          az role assignment create \
            --assignee-object-id "$PRINCIPAL_ID" \
            --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
            --role 'Key Vault Secrets User' \
            --scope "$KV_ID" || true   # idempotent — ignore if already exists
Summary

Managed identities operate within Azure RBAC by acquiring tokens that Entra ID validates; those tokens only grant access where a role assignment exists. Each role assignment connects a managed identity's principal ID, a role definition (the permission set), and a scope (the resource boundary). Prefer narrow scopes — individual resources rather than subscriptions — to limit blast radius. Use az role assignment create with --assignee-object-id and --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal for reliable automation. Role assignments propagate in seconds but may take up to 5 minutes; add retry logic in deployment pipelines. Regularly audit assignments with az role assignment list and AzureActivity KQL queries to catch stale or over-privileged grants.
